content.assessment: Viewed comparatively, the status-quo trajectory resembles a governance vacuum unlike almost any comparator system examined: it lacks Scotland's presumption-based procedural safeguard, Wales's federation-consideration duty, France's reciprocal contractual mechanism, or even Austria's managed-closure ritual for unavoidable cases. It most closely mirrors a pre-reform baseline against which comparator countries deliberately legislated. The mechanism of survival-via-informal-mobilization parallels the Hungarian Erdőkürt case and Polish 2000 village protests, but the Polish comparison shows this ad hoc protest route yields only partial, inconsistent success (two of four schools saved) even in a less centralized system than Hungary's, suggesting Hungary's centralized KLIK-successor structure would make informal reversal even less reliable. Transferability of any 'evidence' about this pathway is limited because no comparator deliberately retains this model as policy; it appears elsewhere only as an unaddressed gap.
opportunities: None identified within this lens beyond serving as a baseline against which reform proposals can be benchmarked
strengths: Requires no new legal architecture, so implementation similarity to comparator systems is not a relevant risk since no transfer is being attempted

Continuation of informal reversal has a documented (if inconsistent) analogue in Hungary's own Erdőkürt case and Poland's 2000 protests, showing local mobilization can sometimes work under similarly informal conditions
threats: Uneven, non-rule-based outcomes documented as a general risk are consistent with the mixed and inconsistent Polish protest outcomes, suggesting this pattern is likely to recur rather than resolve under continued informality
Absence of any codified procedure or evidence infrastructure leaves Hungary without the
weaknesses: Lacks any equivalent to Scotland's, Wales's, or France's codified decision procedures, meaning Hungary would remain a comparative outlier in leaving closure entirely to administrative/informal discretion

The centralization documented for Hungary (KLIK, 2013) removes the municipal bargaining power that even the informal Polish protest cases relied on, making comparability to Poland's partial-success outcomes weak
